title: The Cash Flow Question Your Bank Balance Can't Answer
linkedin:
Check the bank balance. Feel reassured. Move on with the day.

That is the entire cash flow strategy for a lot of owner-led businesses, and it cannot see more than a few days ahead.

A balance is a photograph, not a forecast. It shows every payment that has already cleared and says nothing about what is known, scheduled and still to come. Quarterly BAS, the superannuation guarantee, a supplier invoice on 30 day terms. None of it shows up in today's number, yet all of it is entirely predictable weeks in advance if anyone sits down to map it.

We see the same pattern across owner-led businesses again and again. The ones that get caught short are rarely the ones with thin margins. They are the ones whose only cash flow tool is the number on the banking app, checked reactively instead of planned against.

A rolling 13 week view catches three things a static balance never will. Timing lag on debtors, where an invoice looks like cash in the pipeline but is genuinely six weeks from landing given how that customer actually pays. The quiet trading week, where revenue softens but wages and rent keep running at full pace. And the pile up effect, where two or three manageable payments happen to land in the same week and only look serious once viewed together.

The start of a new financial year is a good moment to build the habit. The prior year has just closed, EOFY pressure has passed, and there is a natural pause before the next quarter's obligations start landing.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance #BusinessOwners
facebook:
Quick one for the business owners here: when did you last look past today's bank balance to what's actually landing in the next few weeks?

A healthy number this morning doesn't mean much if BAS, super and a couple of supplier payments are all due the same week you haven't looked at yet. Most cash flow surprises we come across aren't really surprises. They're just payments nobody mapped out ahead of time.

Do you check the balance daily, or do you actually know what the next month looks like? Genuinely curious where most owners sit on this one.

#CashFlow
google:
A note for local business owners as the new financial year settles in. Checking the bank balance each morning feels like cash flow management, but it only shows what has already cleared. It says nothing about the BAS payment, superannuation guarantee or supplier invoice already due in the next few weeks.

We see this pattern often with owner-led businesses across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria. The businesses caught out by a tight week are rarely the ones with weak margins. They are usually the ones relying on today's balance instead of a forward view of the weeks ahead.

A simple weekly habit fixes most of this: mapping known payments and expected receipts against the actual week they land, not the day the invoice was raised. Once that view exists, a tight week stops being a surprise and becomes something you can see coming and plan around, well before it arrives.

The start of a new financial year is a good time to set this rhythm up, while EOFY reporting pressure has passed and before the next quarter's obligations start landing.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
